title: 👑 Elite Challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: Nuance and Confusable Words

questions.prompt:
Read: "The policy change effected a significant reduction in carbon emissions."
Which statement about the use of "effected" is CORRECT?

options.text: It is wrong — it should be "affected".
It is wrong — it should be "had an effect on".
It is correct — "effect" can be used as a formal verb meaning to bring about or cause.
It is wrong — "effected" is not a real English word.

questions.explanation: "Effect" can function as a rare, formal verb meaning to bring about or cause: "to effect a change" = to make a change happen. The sentence correctly uses "effected a significant reduction" to mean brought about a reduction. "Affected" would mean had an impact on (a different meaning). This is a genuine C2-level distinction taught in the cours.

questions.prompt: Find the sentence with the SUBTLEST collocation error.

options.text: The study provides strong evidence for the link between diet and health.
She gave a deep speech about her experiences.
The committee reached a unanimous decision.
He raised a valid concern about the budget.

questions.explanation: "Gave a deep speech" is the error. The adjective that collocates with speech in this context is "profound", "moving", "powerful", or "heartfelt" — not "deep". "Deep" collocates with thought, sleep, breath, or concern, but not speech in this way. (a) strong evidence, (c) unanimous decision, and (d) raised a concern are all standard collocations.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The historical victory of 1966 is still celebrated today. It was a historic moment for the nation."
Are these two uses of "historic" and "historical" correct?

options.text: Yes, both are correct — they mean the same thing.
No — both should be "historic".
No — "historical" in the first sentence is wrong; "the historic victory" is correct. "Historic" in the second sentence is also correct.
No — "historic" in the second sentence is wrong; it should be "historical".

questions.explanation: "Historical" means relating to or belonging to history in general (a historical novel, historical records). "Historic" means significant or landmark — something that makes history. A victory in 1966 that is still celebrated is a landmark event: the correct adjective is "historic". The second use (a historic moment for the nation) is correct. So the first sentence uses "historical" incorrectly — it should be "historic".

questions.prompt:
Read: "The committee ___ seven members: three from the legal sector, two economists, and two scientists."
Choose the MOST formally precise option.

options.text: is comprised of
is made up of
is composed by
comprises

questions.explanation: "Comprises" is the formally preferred active form: the whole (committee) comprises its parts (the members). "Is comprised of" reverses the passive incorrectly in prescriptive formal usage — many style guides reject it. "Is made up of" is informal. "Is composed by" uses the wrong preposition; the correct passive is "is composed of".

questions.prompt: Which sentence contains a CONNOTATION error — a word whose implied feeling contradicts the intended positive meaning?

options.text: She is renowned for her compassionate approach to patient care.
He is celebrated for his innovative research methods.
The professor is notorious for inspiring generations of students.
The architect is admired for her minimalist design philosophy.

questions.explanation: "Notorious" implies fame for something bad or disreputable. Inspiring students is a positive quality — the correct words would be "renowned", "celebrated", or "admired". The other three sentences use words with positive or neutral connotations that correctly match the positive qualities described.

questions.prompt:
Read: "Despite her outwardly ___ demeanour, she was deeply ___ to the suffering around her — often moved to tears by small acts of injustice."
Choose the BEST pair.

options.text: sensitive / sensible
sensible / sensitive
disinterested / continuous
notorious / sensible

questions.explanation: The contrast "outwardly [calm/practical] demeanour" vs "deeply [emotionally reactive] to suffering" requires: sensible (showing good, practical judgement — the outward appearance) and sensitive (easily and deeply affected emotionally — the inner reality). This contrast is exactly what the confusable pair teaches. The other options are semantically incoherent in context.
